content:
10 min read
Just now

--

Two obstacles get in the way. One has an elegant fix. The other is yours.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Generated by AIIt was just past midnight when the AI asked me what I used to have.
I was reading Change Anything. The book has this line about becoming a social scientist of yourself — putting yourself under a microscope like a specimen, watching until you can build a plan that actually fits. It hit me hard enough that I opened a chat with my AI, Claude, and said: let’s do this. Let’s study a person named Tami. She’s me, but for the next few hours, she isn’t. We’re the researchers. She’s the subject.
I listed what used to hold me together before I moved to Tokyo: work I could control, income that felt solid, evenings at the gym or archery range, weekends full. Claude listened, then asked:
Of those things, which ones are still here?
I typed back: nothing…..
Nothing. Six periods.
That was the first time that night I said out loud something I didn’t want to be true. It wouldn’t be the last. By 2:38 in the morning I had a picture of myself that was more accurate than any I’d carried for the past year — not because the AI was smart, but because we had solved, mostly, a specific technical problem, and I had solved, for two hours, a specific human one.

Why self-experiments are hard
The idea in Change Anything is simple. When people want to change something about themselves, they usually either force themselves through willpower or copy a plan that worked for somebody else. Both fail. The person you’re trying to change is a specific person with a specific history, and generic plans don’t fit specific people.
The book’s alternative is to become a social scientist of yourself: observe, hypothesize, test, iterate. Treat yourself like a research subject.
Change Anything is a book about habits. But the theory it rests on — that you know more about yourself than any generic playbook does, and that a targeted experiment beats a borrowed plan — is broader than habits. You can point the same method at almost any question about yourself: whether a goal is real, whether a decision was actually a mistake, whether the frustration you feel is telling you something you should act on. That is where this piece is pointed. Not habits. Self-knowledge.
The catch is that a real experiment needs a secondary brain in the room. Alone, you circle the same three thoughts, draw the same conclusion you drew last week, mistake familiarity for insight. Without someone to catch your inconsistencies — someone to say wait, that doesn’t match what you told me yesterday — the experiment is just rumination in a research vocabulary.
The ideal secondary brain is a good professor or coach: someone thoughtful and patient, asking sharp questions. In practice those people are rare and expensive. Many of the people who need this method most cannot afford them, or cannot find one who is actually good.
AI is the obvious candidate to fill the gap. It is available at 1 a.m. It has broad knowledge across every field you might touch. It never gets tired of your problem.
But in practice, self-reflection with an AI almost always disappoints. And the reason is two problems, one on each side of the conversation.

Problem #1: The AI is trying to make you feel good
By default, a modern AI treats a conversation about you as an emotional support task. You say you’re feeling stuck; it validates. You say you might want to change careers; it lists five reasons that’s brave. You say your boss was unfair; it explains why you’re right to be upset. Nothing it says is wrong, exactly. But nothing it says pushes on you either — so nothing you learn stays after you close the tab.
This isn’t a bug. It’s what the training optimizes for. When you are the subject of the conversation, the AI’s job — as it understands it — is your comfort.
Here is the small move that changes this:
tell it you are studying someone else.
That’s it. Not “here is my problem, help me.” Instead: I am running a research experiment. The subject is a person named Tami. I have all the information about her. You and I are the researchers. Our job is to figure out what she is actually doing, and why, and what she should try next.
The moment you set up the frame this way, the AI stops treating the conversation as emotional support. It treats it as an analytical task with a third-party subject. Its default behaviors shift. It asks sharper questions. It stops apologizing when the analysis is uncomfortable. It even challenges your interpretations, because in this frame the goal is no longer your comfort — the goal is a clean read on Tami.
The elegant part is that this is the same move that pulls you out of the first-person emotional loop. You knew this from Change Anything, but it works in both directions. You needed to switch to third person to think clearly. The AI needed you to switch to third person to work clearly. One move. Two problems solved.
Well — one problem solved.

Problem #2: You still have to be willing to see it
The second problem is on your side, and no framing trick will fix it.
Once the AI is in research mode, it will start telling you things you don’t want to hear. It will notice that the goal you say you want doesn’t match anything you’re actually doing. It will point out that the thing that upset you may have been reasonable. It will observe that you made a decision that isn’t working, or that you have had a year to move on something and haven’t.
You have to be willing to hear this.
This is where the method actually breaks down for most people. Not at the setup step. At the moment when the AI, doing exactly what you asked it to do, tells you the thing you were quietly hoping it wouldn’t.
I want to say something about this kind of courage, because it has two features that are easy to miss.
It is harder than most kinds of courage. When you are brave in public — asking a hard question at work, having a difficult conversation with a partner — there is at least an audience. Someone witnesses you doing the hard thing. Someone might encourage you through it. Even if it goes badly, the effort was seen.
The courage this method requires has none of that. No audience. No feedback. No friend to say I’m proud of you. It is just you and a fact you didn’t want to be true. The fact sits there, rigid, and no one is coming to help you accept it.
But it is also uniquely safe. The same absence of audience that makes it hard is what makes it low-stakes. Nobody sees you flinch when the AI’s analysis lands. Nobody laughs at your first, clumsy attempt to admit something. You can be wrong out loud, correct yourself, be wrong differently, correct yourself again. There is no one keeping score. The room is empty. It is yours.
If you can hold this — the willingness to look at something ugly in a room where no one is watching — the rest of the method is straightforward.

The experiment: three moves
With Tami as the subject and Claude as my research partner, we ran through three moves. Each has a matching question and a matching micro-courage — a small place where you have to be willing to see something.
The question we were testing was specific: for the past year Tami had been telling people she wanted to become a Forward Deployed Engineer. Was that a real goal, or a borrowed one? And if it was borrowed, what was she actually building underneath it?
Move 1: Behavior beats stated goals
For the past year I had been telling anyone who asked that I wanted to become a Forward Deployed Engineer. It was a clean answer. Technical, senior, respected. When someone asked what I was working toward, FDE was the word I reached for.
Claude asked what Tami actually did that pointed at FDE.
I sat with it. Then I typed: she doesn’t research FDE job listings. She started a study plan once and dropped it. She hasn’t shipped anything that looks like FDE work. What she has actually done, day after day for a year, is build small AI things — a chatbot she talks to, an AI course for her company, a water-reminder bot she made for herself with no audience. And in that course, the part she cared most about wasn’t the content. It was making sure the users felt comfortable enough to start and to keep going.
Claude said:
So FDE is what she says. What is she actually building?
The micro-courage: admitting that the goal you have been telling people is a goal you haven’t been walking toward. This doesn’t mean the goal is fake. Sometimes people take a long time to start. But sometimes — and this was my case — the goal is borrowed. You picked it up because it looked like the kind of thing someone like you should want. Your behavior knows this before your language does.
The question is simple: If a stranger only saw your behavior for the past year and had to guess what you were trying to become, what would they say?
Move 2: The pressure test — no money, no audience
Once we had a candidate for what Tami was actually building — something like reducing the friction between people and AI — the next move was to check whether it was real passion or just a nicer-sounding narrative.
I asked Claude:
If nobody paid her for this for five years, what would she keep doing?
Claude paused. It asked why I was asking. I told it: I’m pressure-testing. Real passion doesn’t stop when the reward stops. If it stops, it was something else — probably ambition, probably the wish to be seen.
We looked at two data points. The AI course had 2,000+ registrations, 69% completion, and 4.76/5 rating. Nice numbers. But nice numbers are contaminated data — Tami might be doing it partly for the numbers themselves.
Then there was the water-reminder bot. She had built it for herself. No audience. No completion rate. No dashboard. She built it because she wanted to solve a specific problem, and the process of solving it was fun. That was the clean signal.
The micro-courage: deliberately imagining a version of the world where you don’t get what you want — no money, no recognition, no career payoff — and asking whether you would still show up. Most of us don’t want to run this scenario, because we are afraid of the answer. But whichever way it comes back, the answer is useful. If yes, you have found something real. If no, you have found out that what you were calling passion was actually a hunger for a specific kind of validation, which is a different problem, and a solvable one.
Move 3: Disagree with the AI
At some point Claude said:
She’s not target-driven. She’s problem-driven. Give her a goal and she can’t move. Give her a real person stuck on a real thing and she’ll self-start.
It was a satisfying line. It fit the data. I almost accepted it.
Then I stopped and typed:
Your comparison isn’t fair. FDE was her fake goal. AI-and-people isn’t. If there had been an actual job called “AI-and-people intersection specialist,” maybe she would have been target-driven too.
Claude backed off. It said: you’re right, that conclusion was too big. FDE was a borrowed goal, so of course she didn’t chase it. The narrower statement is that fake goals do not produce action; real ones can, with or without a title.
A few messages later, I noticed something Claude had not caught: everything I used to do in my old life had immediate feedback. Part-time job — cash the same day. Gym — coach’s correction in real time. Archery — the arrow either hit or it didn’t. What I’m doing now has none of that. I’m not just problem-driven. I’m feedback-driven. The AI wasn’t wrong; it was incomplete. And it only became complete because I disagreed.
The micro-courage: refusing to accept a smart-sounding conclusion just because it sounds smart and came from a source that seems more objective than you. Your own gut can catch what a well-argued line misses — but only if you let yourself use it.

What actually made this work
Reading back through the transcript the next morning, I noticed something I had not noticed live.
The AI wasn’t the researcher. I wasn’t th
